speaker: interviewee
text: French zone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the French zone. They stayed there, the French zone, a couple months. One day, French policemen came in. And they need that building. They take away us, and they're going to send us somewhere else. That time, we were scared, from the concentration camp for-- and we didn't believe it. Maybe they going put us in another concentration camp. Some people are jumping from the second floor, third floor-- running away. They don't want to go with the French people. We don't know what is. We were hiding in the apartment. And we don't go down. We're staying there, and they're howling, come down. Come down. We don't going down. All of a sudden, came in a American [NON-ENGLISH]. That's a British guy. Somebody let him know.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came in with the reporters. And they make pictures where people jumping from the-- from the windows. And then they come since the photographs, say, what do you want from the guys? You don't-- you find another building. Let them stay there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the French police let us stay. And that rabbi, he was a military. He says, now Eden, let's go pray. And he had a field-- a field Torah, with everything. We went in the factory. And all the Jews, we pray that time-- that time. Praise God that we stay there, and he saved us. But we stayed there a couple months. And then they say, Americans, send us-- send you in the American zone. So you better be in-- in Berlin, there was American zone, too. So we went in another place they called Schlachtensee. We went there. So the mother was all the woman, they give her first. They give her-- she went with sister. Me and her and my wife that time, we have to wait our turn.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed there another, maybe, a couple weeks. And then we went on [NON-ENGLISH]. We took a train to go on the horn on the west-- on the west side. So we have pass the Russian zone. They look for documents. We don't have anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow, we came back. And that town was Eschwege. We came back to the little town, Eschwege. It was a camp-- DP camp. There was 4,000 people there. And the mother was there already in her room. And she gave us a place. And we stay there. We stay in Eschwege four years, waiting for papers to go to United States. And over there was born my first son, Harry.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would like to tell us when you got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We-- we were on the-- when we came-- like I told you, we came into Lodz. I was getting food and changing for clothing. And I'm making some kind of living. Then we are talking, we have to run away. I says, I'm not-- I don't want to run away by myself-- I'm by myself. I says to my wife, we have to get married. Then we run away, we are couple.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I made a wedding in Lodz. I invite 30 guests. I invite 30 guests, I invite a rabbi to make the [NON-ENGLISH] and made-- and make the wedding there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a Saturday night. [NON-ENGLISH] there, and everything else. And then next day, Sunday, I says, now we can go away. And then we went to [NON-ENGLISH], like a told you. From [NON-ENGLISH] went to Berlin. That was my honeymoon-- on the third bed. We were staying there for couple weeks, till we're on the way on the French zone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what year you got married and how--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me-- and how you met Clara.

speaker: interviewee
text: Clara was from my town. We were living in the ghetto in the same house. Like I told you in the ghetto, they give us three days. But I was crying all three days. I didn't care. And then after three days I came in to the ghetto, was everything taken. Me and my father, and then a second cousin with us, too. So I went in to where Clara live. I says, I don't know where to live. I'm already too late, the three days. He let me in. I put a bed in the corner. And we stayed there, me and my father. So I met Clara there. And then when she came-- she came back to Korzec in April. We were already in the town for three or four months already. And she didn't have no clothing. So she had to stay in the village there three or four months-- winter months. When it's warm, she can go away. And then she came in-- I met her in April. I gave her some-- gave her some food, I gave her some clothing. And-- and then later on, she find a place to live. And there I know her. But it was a war. I didn't-- didn't think about getting married. But after the war, in Lodz, I was getting married. And then we run together away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the year and date of your-- of your wedding.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly the day. I think Clara know. She has the ring there. I think it's the 21st of November, 1945. In fact, I'm not sure-- it's so long.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that time I've had a wedding there. And we ran away-- only Jewish wedding. And then I was in Germany, she was pregnant with a child. We wasn't married legally. So my child going to be like a bastard. So I went to the German government. And I married later on, maybe in '48, legally. I was getting married in [NON-ENGLISH], they call it. You mean a state wedding versus a religious wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was-- I had a religious wedding. I didn't have a separate-- later on, I made the papers in '48, maybe a couple months before it was born, we were making the papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Eschwege, like I told you. It was a lot of people-- about 4,000 people. Four years, everybody know each other. And we're all waiting for papers from United State. One time, I did receive paper from my uncle. It didn't include my son. I had a son already. So the papers had to go back. Meantime, the mother and the sister, they went to United State nine months before us. And I have to wait nine months till I get the right papers and everything. And then we went to the Bremen. Over there, we have to wait for a ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meantime, Bremen was a big epidemic for typhus. So if you catch typhus, they just-- they quarantine you for half a year. I'm taking my son every day, 4 o'clock in the morning. He's a baby from-- how old was he? Maybe 9 months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Running through the field, and staying all day in the field in order not to catch the typhus. That's the way we stayed there for two, three months till they gave us a ship. We went. And the ship was called It was going 12 days with the ship, to the United States. On the ship, I had to work. And I-- I registered that I am going to clean up apartments. And my wife had a-- had a room with another girl. So I stay, and I cleaned up apartment. And I clean it, and they bring some water and everything else. And we came in the 23rd-- if I'm not mistaken, I was-- we came into New York and the next day to Chicago in 1949-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you began living in Chicago in 1949.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1949 I began living in Chicago. I came in. And landsman of mine send me out some papers. He had a delicatessen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what can I do by him? I don't know the language. I can wash dishes. And he's going to pay me maybe $0.70, $0.80 an hour.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another landsman of mine brought me to a factory-- auto radiator company to make radiators. And over there, they're going to pay me $1.10 an hour. And you can actually talk. So I want to go there better.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went there to the auto radiator company. And you make parts for the boiler radiators. You know radiators?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed there for six, seven weeks. Six, seven weeks-- after six, seven weeks, no more work. So they took me to another job, cutting-- cutting sheet metal, but copper sheet metal.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying my best. I'm working on the sheet metal. The foreman came in, the guy says, how much did you make today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, I make 500. I thought that's a lot. He bawls me up. That's not enough. I'm getting excited. I'm pained. And he went away. I'm getting so excited, and with pains-- I cut my finger off there at that time. Was a big shear. And with your foot, you have to go down and cut the thing. I put a thing there. At that times, didn't have a-- a safety net-- didn't have. So I put metal on, I cut off a piece of finger. And that, after six, seven weeks-- have to go to doctor, put back. He did me a favor. Doctor wrote it down that I cut the whole finger, they pay me $350 insurance. After that, they make a safety line. You know, just make a line. You shouldn't be able to stick your finger. They didn't have it. That was my first job.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said you made 500, what did you mean? Are you speaking in zloty?

speaker: interviewee
text: 500 sheets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sheets.

speaker: interviewee
text: 500 sheets until 3 o'clock. And this was not enough. He says, this was nothing, he says, you made 500 sheets. So I was getting excited. And I cut my finger off. And I stay in the house a couple months. And I was very, very bad shoes. And my foot hurt me very bad. So I'm looking for something where I can sit down-- a job. I'm going to be a tailor, operator. I'm going to [INAUDIBLE]. And they put me into-- teach me how to make pockets. And they're going to pay me $32 a week, even if I make it, don't make it. It took me a couple months till I learn this-- to make pockets. Then they put me in there. And then I start to work it. And then to patch pockets was very good earning. You could make like $7, $8 an hour. And I was start making $7, $8 an hour. And I was making that time maybe $80, $90 a week. Came in [INAUDIBLE] and say, enough. They brought the machine. They put in the pocket. Just string it on, and then you're going to use the machine. And they you only $0.17 instead of $0.50. And I'm start making again maybe $30 a week again. There was a foreman there. His name was Nathan, too. He said, please, save yourself. Go out from here. Look for something else. I went in after a landsman, I went to-- to Wilson Company, a packinghouse. And they took me in to write orders every day and everyday shipping out cheese. All kind of cheese. They gave me the key to the cheese. They have 70 kind of cheese. And I was working in that Wilson Company-- oh my, God, maybe till I find a little business for myself. Like three years, I worked for them. And then I still make $7. And some people don't make a lot of money.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm, so what should I do now? I'm going to buy a chicken store. On Meadow Street, two stores. One was asking $5,000. I hear a live chicken store. He wants to go out for the business. He wants only $1,500.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went in. I took the live chicken store for $1,500. But I don't know anything from my live chickens. So I'm begging him. You should stay and teach me the business. I'm going to pay him $100 a week. He say to me, he stay four weeks. He'll teach me the business. Meantime, we had a order from a church. They say they want capons. They want roosters. They want hens.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I ordered it. They came in live. I says, well, what's a capon? What's a rooster? I don't know. He says, I don't know either. I says, well, I'm paying you $100. You don't know what it is? And I let him go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I find out that he was stealing from me-- from the register-- money, extra. I had that time, $3,500. And in two or three months, I was kaput. Only had $500 left. Was crying like a baby. When he went away and didn't steal, I was working. And I was building up. I was building up the business. I was making $800 a week. That store, what was asking $5,000 a month, they were making $800. Later on, they're making $400 a week. They were taking in, not making-- taking in $400 a week. My landlord tell me, you can stay as much as you want. All of a sudden, he throws me out. He wants-- go out from here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, where I'm going to go? I'm making a living already. For one year, I make $5,000. Where I will go? I'm looking for another business. I'm going a couple blocks, my competition. He doesn't even know me. He says to me, are you Jewish? I says, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Talk to the other guy. He-- he means me. And he says, and you come together with him, and you could make a living. But he's a big competition. Any time I'm putting in $0.39 hens, he puts in $0.29. When I put in $0.29, he puts in $0.19.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I bought the other store by him. And I went out a couple blocks. And I stay at the other store for four years, till 1958.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meantime, my son has allergy. Allergy-- but we went to California, he lose allergy. So I'm going to California. I'm so-- sold my business to another Jewish guy. Sold the business, and I'm going to go out to California, maybe look for a business.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in '58, I came in here to California. Looking for a-- right away, I bought a live-chicken store-- a small one. And just making a living.

speaker: interviewee
text: It comes in New Year's-- Jewish New Year's, and Yom Kippur, I'm closing up the two days. I didn't know that's a-- I was taking in the Saturday, like, $350, $400. Next week, cut in half. They find out that I'm Jewish. Nobody comes into my store. Instead of taking in $400 a week, I'm taking in $200 a week. So I had to close it up. I'm looking for another one. Another one wants $20,000. So I say, OK, I'm going to check it. I'm checking. A week later on, he says, I've changed my mind. I don't want to sell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I'm going to do now? We had two kids. I went back to Chicago. And I bought a concession at K Market. And I stay there 22 years in the K Market.

speaker: interviewee
text: They like me very much because I paid them 10%. But I was like a watchman for them. I was looking up in the papers about them. One time, if you want to hear a story, a gypsy guy came in-- a thief. He took some records-- the best records-- 10 of them. Put it in a shopping bag. He takes it out. He walks it out. I says, I'm calling, and says, I saw that. Better put it back. Instead coming back to me, he runs out in the street. I'm running after him. This was not my job. But I'm get him with the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], with the apron. I'm running in, and I'm hollering, catch him. He's a thief! Two telephone guys working running after him, too. He drop the records. So I'm collecting the records. Then the detective came in, too. He's caught the guy. He caught the guy. He was in jail. Then caught-- went out-- and he run away to California. After six months, they caught him. They came back here jail. I stay there in K Market for, like I told you, 22 years. One time I went away, they had a hold-up, too. So the-- when I- when you're in the back, they are afraid of him. And I know the people. So they never had hold-ups.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they kept me there for 22 years. When I was 57, I sold it to another guy. And what I'm going to do now? I'm 57 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school. Went to school to teach-- to learn myself to be a real estate man. It took me a couple months. I learned it. One time I fall through. By the third time I made it. And I get my real estate license. And I was been in a real estate license. One time I bought a building full of apartment. And I convert it to condos. And I fix it up myself, and I sold it. And that's the way I made a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you came to California.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I came here-- yeah. No, not-- I came to California in-- about seven years ago. I was there in Chicago. I was trying to make good for people-- the Russian people came here. So I was a president of their club, and I try to give them jobs, try to make entertainment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, [NON-ENGLISH]. See the [NON-ENGLISH]? They give me a [NON-ENGLISH]. That says I donated. I had been there-- I was 62. They gave me-- make a party. So it was a committee. And the American government gave us $10,000 for entertainment. So I was the president. I was sign it, and it's another guy sign it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we used to bring in concert-- all kind of concert. Every concert, we didn't make enough, the rest of them fighting with me, we should give them all the money what we collect for the concerts. And I says, well, you need 10% for us-- for the club-- Club Freedom. But I didn't give. All of a sudden, one time, eight people  has to come in for the concert. From Paris, a guy, who take $20 a ticket. I was already meantime coming back, in the desert [INAUDIBLE]. Special, I come back, March the 18 for the concert.

speaker: interviewee
text: I come in. I said, eight people. Only three gypsies making a concert. I says, for them you collect $20? Give them back their money. And all the public is howling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I find out they gets backs-- kick back from the-- from the committee, to get kick back. So I went to Jewish agency. And I told them that. They let go the-- the committee. I have left $2,500. I went into the council. I gave them the money for the Jewish orphan. And they wrote me a letter. They took the money. And that was no more club. And after that, another couple year, I went that year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you choose Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle did. My uncle sent me out papers. My uncle was living in Chicago. He was a liquor salesman. But he was a poor guy. He came in in '38 to Chicago. He was put himself through. And he was stingy, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he sent me papers. So I have to come in there to Chicago. That's how I choose. I didn't choose my own choosing. That's the papers was. Now, your family did not survive. Would you tell me what happened to each member of your family-- your brother, your sister, your mother, and your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandfather. Would you like to start with your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'll start from the beginning. Like in Tisha B'Av, my smaller-- smaller brother, Leo, they took him away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell me the dates? Tell me that story with the dates?

speaker: interviewee
text: The dates?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. When you say Tisha B'Av--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tisha B'Av, that's-- I'm not sure. That was, I think it's 8 of August, 8 of August, 1941, they took him away. But I didn't know that time. And then when they made the big killing in '42 in [NON-ENGLISH]-- that was in April, that that time they took away my mother. They took away my sister. My grandfather was 78 years old. My grandfather-- and he is married the second time-- his wife, too. They all took away. Me and my father was left. And then after three days, they made the ghetto. So I stayed with my father in the ghetto till-- I-- my-- till the second [NON-ENGLISH] that was in September, and I lost him. When I went to school to clean myself, and he went to the ghetto. And they find him in the ghetto. And I started hiding myself in the woods. But I didn't know nobody. I didn't know no streets. I didn't know villagers. And somehow I have to save myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say you-- your brother was taken away, would you describe that? Was it a-- what were the reasons they took him away? And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was no reason. Police-- Ukrainian police was getting young Jewish guys. They were going from street to street, from house to house, and taking away the-- so my mother watches the window. If she comes in the street, the police, I'm running away to the garden. My brother was working that day by a Christian guy, making an oven with another Jewish guy and an older guy-- three. Matter of  fact, she wants to send me there, too. And my neighbor says, I cannot take anymore. Three guys is good enough. But somebody saw them. And they send the police. And they took the two young guys from my neighbor. They took them away. Me, when they went in the garden, they was running after me. I jumped the scale to the stones-- the river-- run away. And I save myself this way. And my father at that time was saved in a bush. He came in a bush and nobody saw him. And that's the way we survived that day. That day they collect 450 people in a day. That time, I didn't know about they kill him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they say he was going to be doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: They say they're taking a camp. They take them away for working. And that's all. We didn't have no mail. We didn't have no letters. I was young. We didn't have no organization. And we thought that maybe it's true. They take them away somewhere to work. That's the way they said he was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then Tisha B'Av of 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, '41-- that was they took away Leo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, excuse me, she wrote down 1941, or '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [NON-ENGLISH] was-- [NON-ENGLISH] were '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH] was, at 4 o'clock in the morning, I hear someone's knocking in the walls. I waked up, and I go down to the halls and looked to the-- to the wall. And I see SS knocking neighbor's door to open up. I jumped into my house. And I says, SS is looking for Jews. And I barefooted, only a pair of pants and a shirt, I run into the garden and try to run to the river. In the garden stays police-- Germans and with guns. And he shoot. And don't ask questions. Shoot straight at me-- one time, twice. See, he didn't say a word to me, just start shooting. So I fall down. And he thought that I was killed. And he came  near to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I looked around. And he says to me, what? Are you not dead yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then came a younger guy-- a German guy, too. And he start beating me up. And they find my father, too, and another guy. And they took us all three up to the-- to the upstairs where all the people was there, not far-- maybe four or five blocks. That's the way where that time that was Tisha B'Av. And that day, they took my-- my mother, and my sister separate. And then my grandfather, they caught him later on. I saw him he-- sitting on a piece of stick. He had a walking stick. He was sitting on the stick. And they didn't tell him to go. They didn't tell him to stay there. Then later on, they told him to sit down, too. And then they took them away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that day was my mother and my sister and my grandfather and his wife. That night, they killed 2,500 people. Let's cut here.
